turns.content: I'm getting this panic when trying to convert my iterator: cannot borrow mut immut in a mutable way. I tried to call map then collect into a Vec but something about the ownership is breaking me here.

That error usually happens because you are trying to mutate an immutable reference or misuse how iterators borrow data. The core issue with lazy evaluation in Rust is that adapters like map and filter don't actually create intermediate collections until you force them. If you chain multiple adapters without calling collect, there's no real allocation happening yet, but if you try to modify the source while iterating over it, you hit this borrow checker panic.
Wait so I can just keep chaining filters and maps forever without any memory cost? That sounds too good to be true. What exactly is happening under the hood when I do map then filter on an iterator?

You are correct that there's no intermediate allocation until you force evaluation, but the chain itself does create new iterator objects for each adapter. When you call map on your original data stream, it returns a new iterator type that wraps the old one and applies the function lazily. Only when you finally call collect into a Vec or iterate with for_each do those lazy operations actually execute and produce values.
So if I don't call collect, my program just sits there doing nothing? That seems weird because I expect some transformation to happen eventually.

Exactly. Without calling collect or consuming the iterator with for_each, the chain remains suspended in lazy state. The transformations you defined are stored as closures waiting to be invoked. You can build arbitrarily deep chains like map then filter then map without any performance penalty until you actually need those results.
But what if I want to stop after the first match? Can I just use for_each to break early?

Yes, that's exactly how you handle it. You can chain your adapters and then call for_each with a closure that returns false when you want to stop processing. This lets you short-circuit the lazy evaluation without needing to build the entire collection in memory upfront.
Okay I get it but wait, what about the performance implications? If I have a huge dataset and only need 10 items, does building that chain still scan everything?

Not if implemented correctly with proper adapters. Standard iterators like map and filter are lazy by design so they won't traverse the entire source unless you force them to. However, some specialized adapters might be eager depending on their implementation, but for basic operations the chain remains truly lazy until consumption.
